title: Planetary Classifications
content:
The DCRA Astrological Survey utilizes planetary classifications to organize planets and planetoids. Various factors are taken into account when classifying a planet, including atmospheric composition, magnetic field, surface temperature, vegetation, and size. Single letter classifications make up base classes, with multi-letter classifications occasionally being designated in addition to the base classes.

Base classes

Class
DescriptionExample
A
Earth-like. Moderately thick oxygen-based atmosphere and capable of having liquid water present on the surface of the planet. Habitable for most humanoid life.[[Terra Astra]], [[Equus]], Nunar
B
No atmosphere and geologically inactive. Typically reserved for small moons and planetoids.Luna, Calisto
C
Thin atmosphere incapable of sustaining liquid water on the surface of the planet; within habitable temperature range of most simple habitats.[[Mars]]
DThick, high pressure atmosphere caused by runaway greenhouse effect.Venus, Titan
EExtremely low average temperature and significant quantity of water. Often referred to as "Ice worlds."Europa
FMarginal exosphere and moderately geologically active.Mercury, Pluto, Ganymede
GModerately thick ammonia-based atmosphere and capable of having liquid water present on the surface of the planet.
HFormerly geologically hyperactive, now inert.Ferax
IGeologically hyperactive. Often referred to as "Lava worlds."Io
JJupiter-like. Large gas giant, >100 km in diameter.Jupiter, Saturn
KNeptune-like. Small gas giant, <100 km in diameter.Neptune, Uranus
LExtreme levels of harmful radiation, either due to lacking a magnetosphere or from other causes.
MExtremely hazardous to even shielded starships. Recommended to be avoided.

Additional designations

T*

Denotes intentionally terraformed worlds. Upon designation, * is replaced with a base classification that best fits the terraforming results. TA designation is the most common. A planet retains it's original base classification, then is followed by a T* designator.

For example, Mars, originally a class-C world, was terraformed into a class-A; so its full classification is C-TA.

L

Historically, L class has been applied to worlds that suffered a full scale nuclear bombardment. With modern methods, the effects of nuclear fallout can be mitigated and reversed, so designating such planets as class-L has fallen out of use.
